title_en: The Doctrine of the Imamate and Its Critique
content_en:
The central theological question dividing the Sunni and Shia traditions concerns the nature and source of legitimate authority after the Prophet Muhammad. The Imami Shia position, as articulated by al-Hilli and the tradition he represented, holds that the Prophet designated Ali ibn Abi Talib as his successor explicitly and publicly, and that this designation carried divine authority. The Imam, in the fully developed Imami theology, is not merely a political leader but a figure of unique religious authority: infallible in his transmission of Islamic law, possessed of esoteric knowledge inaccessible to ordinary Muslims, and the indispensable intermediary between the community and God's ongoing guidance.

Ibn Taymiyyah addresses this doctrine from multiple angles. His first line of argument is textual and historical: he examines the Quranic verses and prophetic hadiths that Shia scholars cited as evidence for the explicit designation of Ali and shows that, when examined within their contexts and evaluated by the standards of hadith criticism, they do not support the weight placed on them. Some of the most commonly cited texts — such as the hadith of Ghadir Khumm, where the Prophet said of Ali whoever takes me as a master also takes Ali as his master — he does not deny as authentic, but he argues that their meaning was misunderstood. The term used does not necessarily imply political succession; it more naturally means friend or ally.

Ibn Taymiyyah's second line of argument concerns the historical record of the early community. He demonstrates at length that the companions of the Prophet — including Ali himself — conducted themselves in ways that are incompatible with the Shia narrative. If Abu Bakr had usurped Ali's rightful caliphate by divine designation, why did Ali give him bay'ah (oath of allegiance) and serve his administration? Why did Ali name sons after Abu Bakr and Umar? Why did the early Imams intermarry with the families of the first caliphs? Ibn Taymiyyah argues that these historical facts, drawn in many cases from sources that Shia Muslims themselves accept, demonstrate that the Shia narrative of a clear divine designation followed by a usurpation was a later construction.

His third line of argument is theological: even granting the importance of the Imamate as an institution, the specific attributes that Imami theology assigned to the Imam — infallibility, esoteric knowledge, cosmic mediation — have no basis in the Quran and Sunnah and represent an inflation of human authority that mirrors what Islam criticizes in other religious traditions. The concept of an infallible religious authority whose pronouncements cannot be questioned or verified resembles the very kind of priestly mediation that prophetic religion had come to replace.

Throughout this analysis, Ibn Taymiyyah is careful to distinguish between different Shia groups and different claims. He does not treat all Shia positions as equally problematic and recognizes gradations within the tradition. His strongest criticism is directed at the fully developed Imami theology of his era, particularly the claims of infallibility and esoteric knowledge, rather than at the general veneration of the Prophet's family, which he considers shared ground with Sunni tradition.
